children.name: Psophodes
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, crested passerines endemic to Australia, characterised by stout, wedge-shaped bills and loud, distinctive vocalisations. They are divided into two groups: the whipbirds, with a characteristic whip-crack song, and the wedgebills, with a softer, bubbling call. They inhabit dense undergrowth, where they forage on the ground for insects and seeds.

children.name: Psophodes olivaceus
children.description: A secretive, olive-green bird with a tall black crest and a white throat, famous for its extraordinary song that ends in a sound exactly like a whip cracking, heard in forests of eastern Australia. The male gives a long, rising whistle and the female instantly follows with the sharp 'crack', a duet that is one of Australia's most iconic bird calls. It skulks in dense undergrowth, more often heard than seen.

children.name: Psophodes nigrogularis
children.description: A greenish-grey whipbird with a black throat and a shorter, less elaborate song than its eastern relative, found in heathlands of southwestern Australia. It has a fragmented distribution due to habitat loss and is considered rare and localised across its range. It forages on the ground in dense heath, often near the edges of wetlands.

children.name: Psophodes occidentalis
children.description: A pale grey-brown bird with a short, wedge-shaped bill and a musical, chiming song that descends in pitch like a set of wind chimes, found across arid and semi-arid Australia. Unlike the whipbirds, it inhabits dry shrublands and saltbush plains, often foraging on the ground in pairs. Its sweet, descending call is a welcome sound in the harsh interior.
